questions.q: What is the direction of the current in a conductor when an electric field is applied?
opts: (D) In the same direction as the electric field
questions.explanation: The direction of the current in a conductor is in the same direction as the electric field because the electric field exerts a force on the free electrons in the conductor, causing them to drift in the direction of the field.

questions.q: What is the unit of resistivity?
questions.explanation: The unit of resistivity is Ohm per meter because it is defined as the ratio of the electric field strength to the current density.

questions.q: A wire of resistance 10 Ohm is drawn to a length double its original length. What is the new resistance?
questions.explanation: When the length of the wire is doubled, the resistance increases by a factor of 4 because resistance is directly proportional to the length of the wire.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a potentiometer in a circuit?
questions.explanation: A potentiometer is used to measure the voltage across a component in a circuit.

questions.q: A battery of emf 2V and internal resistance 1 Ohm is connected to a resistor of 2 Ohm. What is the current in the circuit?
questions.explanation: The current in the circuit can be calculated using Ohm's law and the concept of internal resistance.

questions.q: What is the condition for a potentiometer to measure the emf of a cell accurately?
opts: (A) The current in the potentiometer circuit should be zero
(B) The current in the potentiometer circuit should be maximum
(C) The current in the potentiometer circuit should be minimum
(D) The current in the potentiometer circuit should be finite
questions.explanation: For a potentiometer to measure the emf of a cell accurately, the current in the potentiometer circuit should be zero.

questions.q: What is the difference between emf and terminal voltage?
opts: (A) Emf is the terminal voltage when the circuit is open
(B) Emf is the terminal voltage when the circuit is closed
(C) Emf is the terminal voltage when the current is maximum
(D) Emf is the terminal voltage when the current is zero
questions.explanation: The emf of a cell is the terminal voltage when the circuit is open, i.e., when no current is flowing.

questions.q: A circuit has a resistance of 10 Ohm and a voltage of 20V. What is the power dissipated in the circuit?
questions.explanation: The power dissipated in the circuit can be calculated using the formula P = V^2/R.

questions.q: What is the unit of electric power?
questions.explanation: The unit of electric power is Watt, which is defined as the rate of doing work.

questions.q: A wire has a resistance of 5 Ohm at 20°C and 10 Ohm at 50°C. What is the temperature coefficient of resistance of the wire?
questions.explanation: The temperature coefficient of resistance can be calculated using the formula α = (R2 - R1)/(R1 * (T2 - T1)).

questions.q: What is the purpose of a rheostat in a circuit?
questions.explanation: A rheostat is used to vary the resistance in a circuit.

questions.q: A battery of emf 3V and internal resistance 2 Ohm is connected to a 6 Ohm resistor. What is the current in the circuit?
questions.explanation: The current in the circuit can be calculated using Ohm's law and the concept of internal resistance.

questions.q: What is the condition for the maximum power to be dissipated in a circuit?
opts: (A) The load resistance should be equal to the internal resistance
(B) The load resistance should be greater than the internal resistance
(C) The load resistance should be less than the internal resistance
(D) The load resistance should be zero
questions.explanation: For the maximum power to be dissipated in a circuit, the load resistance should be equal to the internal resistance.

questions.q: A circuit has two resistors of 4 Ohm and 6 Ohm connected in parallel. What is the equivalent resistance of the circuit?
questions.explanation: The equivalent resistance of the circuit can be calculated using the formula 1/R = 1/R1 + 1/R2.

questions.q: What is the difference between series and parallel circuits?
opts: (A) In series circuits, the current is the same in all components, while in parallel circuits, the voltage is the same across all components
(B) In series circuits, the voltage is the same across all components, while in parallel circuits, the current is the same in all components
(C) In series circuits, the resistance is the same in all components, while in parallel circuits, the resistance is different in all components
(D) In series circuits, the power is the same in all components, while in parallel circuits, the power is different in all components
questions.explanation: In series circuits, the current is the same in all components, while in parallel circuits, the voltage is the same across all components.

questions.q: A circuit has three resistors of 2 Ohm, 3 Ohm, and 4 Ohm connected in series. What is the equivalent resistance of the circuit?
questions.explanation: The equivalent resistance of the circuit can be calculated using the formula R = R1 + R2 + R3.

questions.q: What is the concept of internal resistance of a cell?
opts: (A) It is the resistance of the cell when it is not connected to any circuit
(B) It is the resistance of the cell when it is connected to a circuit
(C) It is the resistance of the circuit when the cell is not connected
(D) It is the resistance of the cell when the current is maximum
questions.explanation: The internal resistance of a cell is the resistance of the cell when it is connected to a circuit.

questions.q: A battery of emf 4V and internal resistance 1 Ohm is connected to a resistor of 2 Ohm. What is the terminal voltage of the battery?
questions.explanation: The terminal voltage of the battery can be calculated using the formula V = E - Ir.

questions.q: What is the concept of meter bridge?
opts: (A) It is a device used to measure the resistance of a wire
(B) It is a device used to measure the voltage across a component
(C) It is a device used to measure the current in a circuit
(D) It is a device used to measure the power dissipated in a circuit
questions.explanation: A meter bridge is a device used to measure the resistance of a wire.

questions.q: A wire has a resistance of 10 Ohm at 0°C and 15 Ohm at 100°C. What is the temperature coefficient of resistance of the wire at 0°C?
questions.explanation: The temperature coefficient of resistance can be calculated using the formula α = (R2 - R1)/(R1 * (T2 - T1)).

questions.q: A circuit has two resistors of 3 Ohm and 6 Ohm connected in parallel. What is the equivalent resistance of the circuit?
questions.explanation: The equivalent resistance of the circuit can be calculated using the formula 1/R = 1/R1 + 1/R2.

questions.q: What is the condition for the maximum current to flow in a circuit?
opts: (A) The load resistance should be equal to the internal resistance
(B) The load resistance should be greater than the internal resistance
(C) The load resistance should be less than the internal resistance
(D) The load resistance should be zero
questions.explanation: For the maximum current to flow in a circuit, the load resistance should be zero.
